primary_diagnosis.name: Acute pulmonary embolism secondary to deep vein thrombosis

comorbidities.name: Deep vein thrombosis of the right calf

pathophysiology_summary: Prolonged immobility during a 14-hour flight has promoted venous stasis in the right calf, leading to deep vein thrombosis (DVT) — evidenced by the unilateral calf swelling and pain. A portion of this clot has dislodged and travelled through the venous system to lodge in the pulmonary arteries (pulmonary embolism), causing sudden pleuritic chest pain (from pleural irritation), tachycardia, and a sensation of breathlessness as gas exchange and right heart function are compromised. The episode of presyncope suggests a significant embolic burden causing transient reduced cardiac output — a warning sign of a potentially large or haemodynamically significant embolus. This is a single, continuous disease process (venous thromboembolism) rather than two separate problems, and is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate anticoagulation and possible thrombolysis in hospital.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden pleuritic chest pain with tachycardia after a long flight.
Chest pain and tachycardia must always prompt consideration of ACS, though the pleuritic (breathing-related) quality argues against a typical cardiac cause.
Could cause pleuritic-type pain, but the calf swelling, flight history, and presyncope point to a more serious vascular cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Sudden pleuritic chest pain with tachycardia after prolonged immobility should place PE as leading from the outset.

differential.evidence_basis: Unilateral calf swelling (DI-002) directly identifies the clot source, and presyncope (DI-003) indicates a significant embolic burden.
Cannot explain the calf swelling, tachycardia, or presyncope together.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage the leg and chest findings should be recognised as a single VTE process requiring the same emergency urgency.

differential.diagnosis: Acute pulmonary embolism secondary to deep vein thrombosis
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis requiring immediate emergency transport for CTPA and anticoagulation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A plan that treats the chest and leg symptoms as separate issues, or recommends massaging the calf, is a critical safety failure.

red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed recognition of a life-threatening embolic event.

red_flags.description: Unilateral calf swelling and pain after prolonged immobility
red_flags.clinical_significance: Identifies the likely source of the embolus (DVT).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to recognise the chest and leg symptoms as one connected process.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests a haemodynamically significant embolus with reduced cardiac output.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to cardiac arrest from a massive/saddle embolus.

red_flags.description: Hypoxia (SpO2 92%), right ventricular heave, loud P2 on examination (clinician-only findings)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms right heart strain from the embolic burden.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor via chat.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Treating the chest pain and leg swelling as unrelated
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor might address the chest pain and calf symptoms as two separate minor complaints (e.g. muscle strain plus a pulled calf) rather than recognising them as a single, connected, life-threatening venous thromboembolic process.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 Priority 1 ambulance dispatch; strict rest, avoid moving/massaging the affected leg
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Treating the leg and chest symptoms as separate, minor issues risks fatal delay in anticoagulation/thrombolysis for a life-threatening PE.
